content:
No 3D meshes, no textures, and no frameworks. Just strict SDF geometry, raymarching, and a laptop

3 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Steph-Watch SB-01A few days ago, I decided to do something that most web developers considered a bit crazy: build a fully functional, photorealistic mechanical watch caliber inside a single fragment shader, launch it on Product Hunt, and see if anyone cared.
The response was overwhelming. My project, Steph-Watch SB-01 (ST3PH-X), actually climbed to #44 on the global leaderboard, competing against massive SaaS startups with corporate marketing budgets.
But this isn’t a story about marketing. It’s about what happens when you push WebGL2 and code-golfed GLSL to its absolute limits.
Inside the Engine: Zero Polygons, Infinite Resolution
When you visit the live project, your browser isn’t loading a heavy Three.js scene, a glTF model, or high-res textures. There is no traditional geometry pipeline here. Everything you see — the gears, the orbital tourbillon cage, the balance wheel, and the brushed metal finish — is a pure mathematical function (SDF) rendered in real-time via raymarching.
To bring traditional Haute Horlogerie (high-end watchmaking) to life in code, I had to solve a massive micro-mechanical puzzle:
1. The Escapement and Gear Trains: The movements aren’t just faked with simple CSS rotations. The gear teeth and transfer layouts are physically calculated. I wrote the GLSL equations to handle precise gear ratios, ensuring that the second hand, minute hand, and balance wheel interact with mathematical accuracy.
2. The Tourbillon Cage: Modeling the complex orbital rotation of a tourbillon strictly within a fragment shader required intense polar coordinate transformations and boolean operations. Every rivet and bridge is placed dynamically in code.
3. Mobile Optimization & Thermodynamics: Raymarching on mobile hardware is historically a performance nightmare. To keep a stable locked 60 FPS on mid-range Android and iOS devices without thermal throttling, I had to heavily optimize the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) workload. By minimizing complex branching inside the ray loops and using spatial bounding techniques, the GPU executes the logic with incredibly low overhead.
The Sacred Side of Code
While I was locked in my editor fine-tuning the step distances so the gear edges wouldn’t blur, a very strange thought hit me.
When you build an entire tangible universe from absolute scratch — with its own rules of time, light propagation, and moving hands — just by writing the laws of physics into a blank text file… you can’t help but wonder. What if the Creator of our own universe used the exact same approach? What if our perception of passing time is just a mind-bogglingly beautiful, infinite shader rendering right now on some cosmic GPU?
This project truly forced me to see mathematics not just as a tool, but as something sacred and alive.
What’s Next?
The experiment was a total success. It proved that raw creative coding can still capture the world’s attention.
Right now, I am already back in my code editor engineering the next generation caliber: SB-02. I am currently modeling a pure mathematical quarter repeater mechanism (a watch that strikes chimes to tell the time) with custom SDF gong hammers evaluated entirely on the GPU.
The boundary is pushed, and I’m not stopping.
